sections.title: Derivative Markets and Instruments
sections.content: Derivatives are financial instruments whose value depends on (derives from) an underlying asset. Main types include forwards, futures, options, and swaps.
keyPoints: Options: right but not obligation to buy/sell

practiceQuestions.question: A forward contract is:
options: An option to buy or sell
practiceQuestions.explanation: Forward contracts are customized agreements between two parties to buy/sell an asset at a future date for a price agreed today. They trade over-the-counter (OTC).

practiceQuestions.question: A call option gives the holder the right to:
practiceQuestions.explanation: A call option gives the holder the right (but not obligation) to buy the underlying asset at the strike price.

practiceQuestions.question: Which derivative is typically used to hedge against interest rate risk?
practiceQuestions.explanation: Interest rate swaps are commonly used to hedge against or speculate on interest rate movements by exchanging fixed and floating rate payments.

sections.title: Option Valuation
sections.content: Option pricing depends on several factors including underlying price, strike price, time to expiration, volatility, and interest rates. Intrinsic value and time value make up an option's premium.
keyPoints: Time value: premium over intrinsic value

formulas.expression: Call Intrinsic Value = Max(0, S - X)
Put Intrinsic Value = Max(0, X - S)

practiceQuestions.question: A call option with strike price $50 when the stock trades at $55 has an intrinsic value of:
An out-of-the-money put option has:
practiceQuestions.explanation: A put option is out-of-the-money when the stock price is above the strike price. Intrinsic value = Max(0, X - S) = 0.
